metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: AC2's oracle ('a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value') only verifies value-equality (both == 50), not structural sharing. It would pass even with mirrored constants, which contradicts AC2's own text ('The cap is one named constant governing both paths').
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Update AC2 oracle to: 'a test importing both sidecar modules and asserting `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` (object identity), or asserting both reference the same fully-qualified constant from a shared module.'

findings.finding: AC2's stated oracle ('a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value') is weaker than AC2's stated criterion ('The cap is one named constant governing both paths'). A mirrored-constants implementation (two separate variables both set to 50) satisfies the oracle but violates the 'one named constant' structural claim, meaning the AC can be green while the criterion is false.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'The cap is one named constant governing both plan- and code-review prune paths. Oracle: a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value.'
findings.suggested_fix: AC2 oracle: 'a test asserting `plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET` (import identity), confirming a single definition site; plus `grep -c "RETAIN_PER_TICKET\s*=" src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` should equal 1 (only one assignment exists in the codebase).'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC/oracle that simulates a prune failure (e.g., patch git operations to raise) and asserts `emit()` still completes successfully, validating the exception-swallowing contract on the new code-review prune path.
evidence: Plan text: 'best-effort and exception-swallowing — a failed prune never fails the gate'
Absence: no AC specifies an oracle for the failure/exception path of prune(), meaning a future regression (prune raising instead of swallowing) would not be caught by the test suite
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `prune()` is 'best-effort and exception-swallowing — a failed prune never fails the gate,' but no AC or oracle tests this contract: there is no test that simulates a prune failure (e.g., git rm raises, filesystem error) and asserts that `emit()` completes successfully and the exception is swallowed rather than propagated.
findings.location: Scope section ('best-effort and exception-swallowing') and Acceptance Criteria (all four ACs)
scenarios: A refactor accidentally removes the try/except wrapping prune(); the suite stays green because no test exercises a failing prune
A git rm permission error in production causes emit() to raise, silently breaking the write gate, with no test having validated this contract
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the ACs: 'Oracle: a test that patches the underlying git rm call to raise an exception and asserts that code_review.sidecar.emit() completes without raising and the newly-appended event is present.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit statement to the Compatibility & rollback section that, like plan-review, already-pruned code-review sidecar events (those deleted on the first emit after deploy) are also non-recoverable after rollback.
evidence: 'Already-pruned plan-review history is not recovered (already deleted); going forward more is retained.' — explicit for plan-review
'Code-review sidecars that already grew unbounded will be pruned to 50 on the next emit.' — states the action but not that these deletions are also irreversible post-rollback
Rollback sentence for code-review: 'removes the code-review prune (code-review growth becomes unbounded again)' — omits 'already-pruned code-review events cannot be recovered'
findings.finding: The first-emit pruning of existing unbounded code-review sidecars is a destructive, irreversible step (events above 50 are permanently deleted on the next emit after deploy), but the Compatibility/rollback section only states this explicitly for plan-review history ('Already-pruned plan-review history is not recovered') — the equivalent statement for code-review is absent, leaving the irreversibility of the bulk-prune-on-first-emit implicit rather than explicit.
findings.location: Compatibility & rollback section
scenarios: An operator reverts the change expecting to restore the full unbounded code-review history; the rollback section doesn't warn them the deletions from the first-emit prune are permanent.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the rollback paragraph: 'Code-review sidecar events deleted on the first post-deploy emit are also non-recoverable after rollback, analogous to already-pruned plan-review history.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either extend the shared-import to `completion_sidecar.py` (import `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` from `rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar` there too, raising its bound to 50 consistently), or narrow the plan's rationale to explicitly acknowledge that only the plan+code-review pair is consolidated and that `completion_sidecar.py` retains an independent bound — whichever is intended.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py:34: RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 10 — a third sidecar with the identical value, not mentioned in the plan
Plan quote: 'reference the shared bound (import RETAIN_PER_TICKET from rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar, so there is ONE named constant governing both paths — no second literal to drift)'
After this change there will be TWO literals: plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50 and completion_sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 10 — the latter still drifts independently
findings.finding: The plan's stated rationale for a shared import ('one named constant, no second literal to drift') is only half-applied: completion_sidecar.py has its own independent RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 10 (line 34) that is left as a separate literal after this change. The plan singles out plan_review → code_review sharing while silently leaving a third identical literal behind, making the consolidation inconsistent with its own motivation.
findings.location: Scope section / Approach rationale — 'a single shared constant (imported, not duplicated) keeps the change minimal and makes "governs both paths" literally true'
scenarios: A future author raises the bound again; they update plan_review.sidecar (which code_review imports) but miss completion_sidecar.py, re-introducing the exact drift the plan claims to prevent.
The plan's AC2 oracle ('RETAIN_PER_TICKET is plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET') only tests two modules; the completion_sidecar literal is never covered by this oracle.
findings.suggested_fix: In completion_sidecar.py, replace `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 10` with `from rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar import RETAIN_PER_TICKET` and add it to the Scope section. Alternatively, if the completion sidecar should stay at 10 (different semantics), document this explicitly in the plan's Approach rationale so the partial consolidation is an acknowledged decision, not an oversight.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit DATA LOSS RISK note to the Compatibility & rollback section stating that code-review sidecar files deleted by the first post-deploy prune (those beyond position 50, removed via committed `git rm`) are permanently irrecoverable after rollback, and that rollback only prevents future pruning — it does not restore already-deleted observability history.
evidence: Plan states: 'Reverting sets the constant back to 10 (prunes more aggressively) and/or removes the code-review prune (code-review growth becomes unbounded again); no migration.'
Plan states: 'Code-review sidecars that already grew unbounded will be pruned to 50 on the next emit.'
Plan does NOT state that the pruned (git-rm'd and committed) files are permanently lost post-rollback.
T11 POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0'
findings.finding: The plan's Compatibility & rollback section does not explicitly assert the post-rollback persisted state of code-review sidecar files that were already deleted by the first post-deploy prune. Rollback restores the Python constant and removes the prune call, but any code-review REVIEW_RESULT sidecar files that were `git rm`'d and committed (those beyond position 50) are permanently absent from the tickets branch — rollback cannot recover them. T11 requires the plan to assert on the schema/data STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that the code change is reverted.
findings.location: Compatibility & rollback section
scenarios: A ticket with 200 accumulated code-review sidecars gets 150 pruned on first emit post-deploy. A rollback of the code change leaves those 150 files permanently deleted from the git history of the tickets branch. An auditor expecting historical code-review rounds cannot recover them.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Compatibility & rollback section, add: 'DATA LOSS RISK (observability-only): On the first emit after deploy, any code-review ticket with more than 50 existing REVIEW_RESULT sidecars will have the excess permanently deleted via committed `git rm`. Rollback of the code change does not restore these files — only the prune behavior is reverted, not the already-committed deletions. This is acceptable because the deleted files are reducer-ignored observability records (not state-bearing), but consumers relying on full code-review history depth beyond 50 should be aware.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the concurrent-prune race in the plan (or specify that the best-effort/exception-swallowing semantics explicitly cover the git-conflict case, and that slightly exceeding 50 events under concurrent writes is an accepted outcome).
evidence: 'list the ticket's -REVIEW_RESULT.json events by filename timestamp, git rm all but the newest keep, commit with --no-verify'
ABSENCE: no mention of locking, serialization, or idempotency guard for concurrent callers on the same ticket
findings.finding: The list-files → git-rm → commit sequence in prune() is a TOCTOU pattern on a shared git branch. Two concurrent review processes on the same ticket can each list files, independently decide which to delete, and race on the commit — leaving either a git conflict (both prunes fail silently) or a double-deletion of a file the other process already removed.
findings.location: Scope section — code_review/sidecar.py prune spec ('list the ticket's -REVIEW_RESULT.json events by filename timestamp, git rm all but the newest keep, commit with --no-verify')
scenarios: Two parallel CI jobs triggered by the same PR both finish a review within seconds, both call emit() → prune() concurrently; git push conflict causes both prunes to fail silently, leaving N > 50 events
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a note in the plan: 'Concurrent prunes on the same ticket may both fail with a git conflict; both are swallowed as best-effort. The cap is a soft bound — under concurrent writes the count may transiently exceed 50 until the next emit.'
No code change needed if transient overflow is acceptable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge or resolve the shared EVENT_TYPE='REVIEW_RESULT' collision: the specified code_review.prune() filter `f.endswith('-REVIEW_RESULT.json')` matches plan-review sidecars in the same ticket dir — either document that both prune paths share a single pool of 50 (intentional), or add a payload-schema-based discriminator to separate plan-review vs code-review events during pruning.
evidence: code_review/sidecar.py line 21: EVENT_TYPE = 'REVIEW_RESULT'
plan_review/sidecar.py line 28: EVENT_TYPE = 'REVIEW_RESULT'
plan_review/sidecar.py prune() line 84: `if f.endswith(f'-{EVENT_TYPE}.json')` — same filter the plan prescribes for code_review.prune()
Both sidecars emit to the same ticket directory (via append_event on the same ticket_id / target_ticket)
Plan Scope: 'list the ticket's -REVIEW_RESULT.json events by filename timestamp' — no disambiguation between plan-review and code-review events
findings.finding: Both plan_review/sidecar.py and code_review/sidecar.py define EVENT_TYPE = 'REVIEW_RESULT' (identical string), and both emit to the same per-ticket directory. The specified code_review.prune() filters files by `f.endswith(f'-{EVENT_TYPE}.json')`, which will match plan-review REVIEW_RESULT events stored in the same ticket dir — so code_review.prune() will silently consume and delete plan-review sidecars, and vice versa. The plan does not acknowledge or address this filename-level collision between the two prune paths sharing the same ticket directory and event-type suffix.
findings.location: Scope section — code_review/sidecar.py prune() spec; also src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py:21 (EVENT_TYPE = 'REVIEW_RESULT') vs src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:28 (EVENT_TYPE = 'REVIEW_RESULT')
scenarios: A ticket that has both plan reviews and code reviews (a normal workflow ticket with associated code changes): the code_review prune counts ALL REVIEW_RESULT files (plan-review + code-review combined) against the keep=50 cap, deleting the oldest regardless of source; plan-review prune does the same. Under heavy usage this can prune the 'wrong' sidecar type.
AC1 oracle test ('appending N>50 synthetic events on each path') may not catch this if tests use separate ticket dirs per path — but production always accumulates both on the same ticket.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the directional import (code_review → plan_review) is consistent with the codebase's existing module dependency rules, OR extract RETAIN_PER_TICKET into a shared neutral module (e.g. rebar/llm/review_constants.py) so both sidecars are peers rather than one depending on the other.
evidence: 'import RETAIN_PER_TICKET from rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar, so there is ONE named constant governing both paths'
No mention of a shared constants module or verification that this coupling direction is architecturally consistent with other sibling-module relationships in rebar.llm
findings.finding: The plan introduces a directional cross-component import dependency (code_review.sidecar imports RETAIN_PER_TICKET from plan_review.sidecar) that is acknowledged and justified but not mitigated. A shared constants module (e.g. rebar/llm/review_constants.py) would give both siblings a neutral common dependency rather than making code_review structurally subordinate to plan_review; the plan offers no mitigation and does not confirm this coupling direction is consistent with the rest of the codebase.
findings.location: Scope section — 'reference the shared bound (import RETAIN_PER_TICKET from rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar)'
scenarios: A refactor that renames or restructures plan_review.sidecar (e.g. splitting it) breaks code_review.sidecar's import silently.
A future reader adding a third review type must discover and continue the plan_review → code_review dependency chain rather than finding a neutral shared location.
findings.suggested_fix: Create rebar/llm/review_constants.py containing RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50; have both plan_review/sidecar.py and code_review/sidecar.py import from it. The AC oracle test (`code_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET is plan_review.sidecar.RETAIN_PER_TICKET`) continues to pass if both re-export the same object.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a WARNING-level log (e.g. `logger.warning('code_review sidecar prune failed: %s', exc)`) inside the exception-swallowing block so prune failures are observable without failing the gate.
evidence: 'best-effort and exception-swallowing — a failed prune never fails the gate'
ABSENCE: no mention of logging the caught exception even at DEBUG/WARNING level, no metric increment, no alert path
findings.finding: Prune failures are silently swallowed with no log, metric, or trace specified, making systematic prune failures (e.g. git permission error, branch lock, detached HEAD) invisible to operators. Unbounded sidecar accumulation would resume with no signal.
findings.location: Scope section — code_review/sidecar.py prune spec; Compatibility & Rollback section
scenarios: git permissions change and every prune silently fails — code-review sidecars grow unbounded again with no operator visibility
branch is locked during a deploy window; prune fails on every emit during that window; no alert fires
findings.suggested_fix: In the except block of both prune() functions, add `logger.warning('sidecar prune failed for ticket %s: %s', ticket_id, exc)` before swallowing. This preserves best-effort semantics while giving operators a signal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the G7 scope-containment violation: either (a) update parent db7b to explicitly include code_review/sidecar.py retention in its Scope/ACs (which stales db7b's plan-review attestation and requires re-review before this leaf can proceed), or (b) split the code-review prune addition into a separate leaf under its natural parent epic (b744 / WS4 code-review sidecar), filing only the plan_review/sidecar.py constant raise as a leaf of db7b.
evidence: Parent db7b Scope states: 'retention is instead a dedicated PRUNE (rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.prune) that keeps the most-recent RETAIN_PER_TICKET sidecars per ticket' — scoped entirely to plan_review.sidecar

Parent db7b AC4: 'A retention/cleanup path bounds sidecar growth … a dedicated sidecar.prune (keep most-recent RETAIN_PER_TICKET per ticket, remove older), run best-effort after emit' — no mention of code_review/sidecar.py

Parent db7b What: 'A reducer-IGNORED REVIEW_RESULT event capturing per-criterion verdicts + finding fingerprints + metadata + override justifications' — describes plan-review observability substrate only

The leaf's own §Current state acknowledges code_review/sidecar.py 'has NEITHER a prune() function NOR a RETAIN_PER_TICKET constant' — confirming this is a new out-of-scope addition, not a refinement of db7b's existing scope
code_review/sidecar.py belongs to epic b744 / WS4 (its module docstring: 'Code-review verdict sidecar (epic b744 / WS4)'), a distinct parent lineage from db7b
findings.finding: The leaf adds a second deliverable — adding prune() and retention to code_review/sidecar.py — that is entirely outside its parent db7b's declared scope. Parent db7b is scoped exclusively to the plan-review REVIEW_RESULT sidecar; neither its What, Scope, nor any AC mentions code_review/sidecar.py, a code-review prune path, or the shared constant import. The leaf is delivering something the parent's plan does not contain.
findings.location: Leaf plan §Scope (code_review/sidecar.py bullets) and §Current state; parent db7b §Scope ('rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.prune') and AC4
scenarios: An implementer following db7b's scope expects changes only to src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py; this leaf also modifies code_review/sidecar.py, which is out of db7b's declared scope and would surprise reviewers auditing against the parent contract.
If code_review/sidecar.py retention has a bug or test failure, the leaf's failure mode spills outside the plan_review observability boundary that db7b owns.
findings.suggested_fix: Option (b) is lower-risk: keep this leaf scoped only to raising RETAIN_PER_TICKET in plan_review/sidecar.py (db7b's natural scope), and file a separate story under the code-review epic (b744) to add prune() + import the shared constant to code_review/sidecar.py. The shared-constant import still works across stories — the constant is raised in this leaf first, and the code-review leaf imports it.

findings.finding: The plan asserts a code-review prune path already exists in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` that can be updated to share the `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant, but the file contains no `prune()` function and no `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant — the emit() function never calls prune. The plan's scope says 'ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune (shared or mirrored constant)', treating the prune path as pre-existing, but it must be created from scratch.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` bullet; AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the code_review/sidecar.py scope bullet with: 'Add a `prune()` function (mirroring `plan_review/sidecar.py:prune`) and a `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant (or import it from plan_review/sidecar), and call `prune()` from `emit()` — this path does not currently exist and must be created, not just updated.'

findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` has NO `prune` function and NO `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant — the plan treats the code-review edit as merely aligning an existing constant/prune path, but that prune path does not exist and must be created from scratch.
findings.location: Scope section — `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py`; also AC line 2 ('The cap is one named constant governing both plan- and code-review prune paths')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope entry for `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py`: 'NOTE: this file currently has no `prune` function or `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant — this ticket must add both, modelled on `plan_review/sidecar.py`'s prune implementation, with `emit` calling `prune` after `append_event`.'

findings.finding: The code-review prune path the plan says the constant will 'govern' does not currently exist: `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` has no `prune()` function and no `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant, and its `emit()` call site in `gate_dispatch.py` makes no prune call. Merely adding or importing a shared constant leaves code-review sidecars entirely un-pruned; AC2's 'both prune paths honor the same value' would be vacuously untestable.
findings.location: Scope section — 'src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py — ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune'; also AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Scope entry for `code_review/sidecar.py` to read: 'Add `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50` (or import the shared constant from `plan_review/sidecar.py`) **and** add a `prune(ticket_id, ...)` function mirroring `plan_review/sidecar.prune`, then call it from `code_review/sidecar.emit()` after a successful append — matching the plan-review pattern exactly.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune path' (Scope section), implying code_review/sidecar.py already has a prune mechanism that just needs the constant aligned. But inspecting the file, code_review/sidecar.py has no RETAIN_PER_TICKET constant and no prune() call — its emit() never invokes any pruning. The plan's scope must recognise that the prune path needs to be ADDED to the code-review sidecar, not merely updated.
findings.location: Scope — `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the Scope bullet for code_review/sidecar.py to read: 'Add a prune() function (mirroring plan_review/sidecar.prune()) and call it from emit(), using the shared/mirrored RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50 constant, so both emit paths enforce the same retention bound.'

findings.finding: The code-review sidecar has no prune() function and no RETAIN_PER_TICKET constant; the plan says 'ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune path' but never specifies that a prune() call+function must be ADDED to code_review/sidecar.emit() — making the required structural change ambiguous (add a new prune path vs. merely mirror a constant).
findings.location: Scope section ('src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py — ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune') and AC2 ('a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value')
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the Scope entry for code_review/sidecar.py to: 'Add a `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET)` function mirroring the plan-review prune pattern, call it from `emit()` after `append_event()`, and import/share `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` from `plan_review.sidecar` (or define a mirrored constant and note the two values must stay in sync).'

findings.finding: The plan asserts there is an existing 'code-review prune path' in `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` that needs only a constant update, but the file contains no `prune()` function and no `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant — the prune path must be written from scratch and wired into `emit()`.
findings.location: Scope section: `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` — 'ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune (shared or mirrored constant)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Note: `code_review/sidecar.py` has no existing prune path — this change must add a `prune()` function (mirroring plan_review/sidecar.prune) and call it from `emit()` after `append_event`, in addition to declaring the shared constant.'

findings.finding: src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py contains no prune() function and no RETAIN_PER_TICKET constant — the 'code-review prune path' the plan names as an existing edit target does not exist; wiring it in requires creating both the function and a call from emit(), which is new behavior (CREATION), not an update.
findings.location: Scope section / src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py / AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Update the Scope entry for code_review/sidecar.py to read: 'Add RETAIN_PER_TICKET (shared with or mirrored from plan_review/sidecar.py) and a prune() function modelled on plan_review/sidecar.prune(); wire it as a best-effort call in emit() after append_event — mirroring the plan-review emit() pattern.'

findings.finding: The code_review/sidecar.py file has no prune() function and its emit() makes no prune call — there is no 'code-review prune path' to govern with a shared or mirrored constant. The plan's mechanism for the code-review half (add/share the constant) is incomplete: a constant with no calling code does not bound code-review sidecar growth.
findings.location: What / Scope section — 'src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py — ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune'
findings.suggested_fix: In code_review/sidecar.py: (1) define RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50 (or import it from plan_review.sidecar); (2) add a prune() function identical in structure to plan_review/sidecar.prune() filtering on f"-{EVENT_TYPE}.json"; (3) call prune(target_ticket, repo_root=repo_root) at the end of emit() (best-effort, exception-swallowed), mirroring line 63 of plan_review/sidecar.py.

findings.finding: The code-review sidecar (`src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py`) has no `prune` function and no `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant — confirmed by tool search. The plan's Scope says 'ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune' and AC2 oracles 'a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value,' treating a prune path as already existing. It does not exist. The plan therefore fails to specify: (1) that a new `prune()` function must be authored for this file, (2) where in `emit()` it should be called, and (3) whether it should follow the same best-effort/exception-swallowing convention as the plan-review counterpart.
findings.location: Scope section / `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py` / AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope bullet for `code_review/sidecar.py`: 'Add a `prune(ticket_id, *, keep=RETAIN_PER_TICKET, repo_root=None)` function mirroring `plan_review/sidecar.prune` (same git-rm logic, same best-effort/exception-swallowing pattern) and call it from `emit()` immediately after the `append_event` call, returning `True` on success regardless of prune outcome.'

findings.finding: The plan's Scope and AC2 claim a 'code-review prune path' exists in `code_review/sidecar.py` and needs its constant aligned — but that file has neither a `RETAIN_PER_TICKET` constant nor a `prune()` function. Proposing a 'shared constant governs both prune paths' when one path doesn't yet exist is premature abstraction; AC2's oracle ('a test asserting both prune paths honor the same value') would require adding new production code that the plan never acknowledges as a new addition.
findings.location: Scope section ('src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py — ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune') and AC2 ('The cap is one named constant governing both plan- and code-review prune paths')
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, replace 'ensure the same cap governs the code-review prune (shared or mirrored constant)' with an explicit statement: 'Add a `RETAIN_PER_TICKET = 50` constant and a `prune()` call in `code_review/sidecar.py`'s `emit()`, mirroring the plan-review sidecar pattern.' Then update AC2's oracle to describe the new test accordingly. Alternatively, if code-review growth is not a current concern, remove the code-review prune from scope entirely and narrow AC2 to plan-review only.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
